statement.markdown: You are given an undirected graph with V vertices and E edges. Traverse the graph using Depth-First Search (DFS) starting from a given source node. Print the vertices in the order they are visited. To make the traversal deterministic, visit the neighbors of any node in ascending sorted order.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers V and E, denoting the number of vertices and edges respectively.
The next E lines contain two integers u and v, denoting an undirected edge between u and v.
The last line contains a single integer representing the source node.
statement.outputFormat: Print the visited vertices in the order they are visited, each on a new line.

examples.explanation: DFS traversal starting from node 0. Neighbors of 0 are 1 and 2 (sorted). We visit 1 first. Neighbors of 1 are 0, 3, 4 (sorted). We visit 3, then backtrack, visit 4, backtrack to 1, backtrack to 0, then visit 2. Order is 0 1 3 4 2.
DFS traversal starting from node 0 yields 0 1 2 3.

hints.text: To solve this problem, consider a traversal algorithm that uses a stack data structure to keep track of nodes to visit next. This algorithm is particularly useful for exploring graphs or trees in a systematic way.

The algorithm you're looking for is Depth-First Search (DFS). It works by selecting a starting node (also known as the source node) in the graph and exploring as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. A key component of DFS is the use of a stack to store nodes that are pending visitation.

In the context of the given undirected and unweighted graph represented as an adjacency list, you will need to maintain a set or a boolean array to keep track of visited nodes to avoid revisiting them. The algorithm should start from the given source node, mark it as visited, and then systematically explore its neighbors, marking them as visited and adding them to the stack if they haven't been visited yet.

editorial.title: Depth-First Search Optimal Solution
editorial.intuition: The key observation that unlocks the optimal approach is that by sorting the neighbors of each node, we ensure that the DFS traversal visits nodes in a consistent order, which helps in avoiding revisiting nodes and ensures completeness.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking all possible paths from the source node to all other nodes, which would result in an exponential time complexity. This approach is slow because it does not utilize the structure of the graph and instead treats it as a general search problem.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a Depth-First Search (DFS) algorithm with a sorted adjacency list.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a recursive DFS function that marks each node as visited, adds it to the result list, and then recursively calls itself for all unvisited neighbors. The time complexity of this approach is O(V + E) and the space complexity is O(V + E), making it much more efficient than the brute force approach.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it visits each node exactly once and that it explores all reachable nodes from the source. This can be done by induction on the number of nodes visited.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm on a sample graph can help verify its correctness and illustrate its efficiency.
commonMistakes: Not sorting the neighbors of each node, which can lead to inconsistent traversal orders
Not marking nodes as visited, which can lead to infinite loops
Not using a recursive function to implement the DFS traversal
Not handling the case where the source node is not reachable from any other node
Not considering the time and space complexity of the algorithm
Not using a sorted adjacency list to ensure consistent traversal orders

functionSignature.cpp: void dfs(vector<vector<int>>& graph, int source, vector<bool>& visited);
functionSignature.java: public void dfs(List<List<Integer>> graph, int source, boolean[] visited);
functionSignature.python: def dfs(self, graph: List[List[int]], source: int, visited: List[bool]) -> None:
functionSignature.javascript: var dfs = function(V, E, edges, source);
